name: Jai
summary: Jonathan Blow's Jai (closed beta since 2014): the compiler is a library that the programmer drives, not a black box. `#run` any function at compile time, `#insert` for AST manipulation, compiler message loop for plug-in metaprogramming, dual backends (custom x64 + LLVM) for fast dev and optimised release builds.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Jonathan Blow's **Jai** has been in closed beta since 2014, targeting release alongside *Order of the Sinking Star*. The guiding design is that the compiler is a library that the programmer drives, not a black box that consumes source files.

Jai's design philosophy is unusually metaprogramming-forward: rather than a separate macro system, the language exposes the compiler itself to user code. Build configuration, code generation, AST manipulation, and even data-layout transformations are written as ordinary Jai code that runs during compilation.

sections.title: Core metaprogramming features
sections.content:
- **`#run` any function at compile time.** Result is either baked into the binary as a constant or used to drive compilation. Canonical demos: generating an sRGB lookup table at compile time; running an entire Space Invaders game at compile time and embedding the final state. The compile-time runtime is a bytecode interpreter built into the compiler.
- **`#insert` for generalised AST insertion.** Produces structured code from a compile-time computation and splices it into surrounding context — enables data-layout transformations (e.g., struct-of-arrays from a struct-of-scalars schema) as library code.
- **Compiler message loop.** A proc marked `#compiler` receives messages from the compiler during compilation ("type-checking started," "declaration parsed," "bytecode generated"); the meta-program can inspect, modify, or reject them. This makes the compiler a plugin host accessible as ordinary Jai. The build system itself is a Jai meta-program (`build.jai`) — no separate configuration language.
- **Dual backends.** Custom x64 backend for fast-path dev builds (no optimization, near-instant compile); LLVM for release. The custom backend exists specifically because even LLVM -O0 adds noticeable compile latency on large codebases. Compile-speed target: 1M LOC/second; public demos show 80k LOC compiling in under a second.

sections.title: Design trade-off
sections.content:
The design trade-off is principled: if compile-time code is ordinary language code that can mutate the AST and control the compiler, there's no "metaprogramming language" to learn. The cost is a compiler that must be designed as a library from day one — hard to retrofit.

For language designers, Jai is the existence proof of "compiler-as-library" as a coherent design choice. The trade-off versus more conventional macro systems: more powerful (the metaprogram can do anything) at the cost of less constrained (the metaprogram can do anything). The scope of what compile-time code can affect is, by design, larger than in Rust, Scala, or even Zig.
